roles.name: Integrator (CLI User)
jobs: Bootstrap an existing repository into DevForge via 'devforge init' or 'devforge init --workspace'
Run predefined fixture cycles to validate environment setup ('devforge fixture game_project')
Execute a snapshot cycle against their own project ('devforge snapshot .devforge/devforge.snapshot.json')
Check executor readiness before running live cycles ('devforge doctor')
Read structured cycle summary (cycle_id, dispatch_count, result_statuses) from CLI output
pains: AI-tool CLIs (claude, codex) have inconsistent CLI interfaces that change without notice
Chat-based dev tools lose context across sessions, forcing repeated re-explanation
No structured way to resume a partially completed development cycle after interruption
Hard to see which AI tool did what work and why
gains: devforge init writes all preferences to .devforge/ so subsequent runs require no re-configuration
Wizard-style init hides llm_preferences/pull_policy_overrides behind human-friendly presets (auto/offline/live, balanced/implementation/testing)
Snapshot file provides auditable record of all work packages and their statuses
Fixture cycles with game_project and ecommerce_project let integrators verify setup without a real project

roles.name: Extender (Adapter Implementer)
jobs: Implement a custom ExecutorAdapter for a new AI CLI tool by satisfying the ExecutorAdapter Protocol
Register a custom adapter in EXECUTOR_REGISTRY via register_executor_adapter()
Override per-project executor routing via ExecutorPolicy (by_phase / by_domain / by_role / by_work_package)
Tune context pull budgets via pull_policy_overrides in devforge.project_config.json
Define project-specific knowledge preferences for different archetypes
pains: Executor CLI tools (codex, cline, opencode) have wildly different invocation semantics
Wiring new backends into orchestration frameworks usually requires touching scheduling logic
No standard way to control how much context an AI executor receives per task
gains: ExecutorAdapter Protocol (src/devforge/executors/base.py:62-109) provides 11 explicit method contracts — implement and register, done
SubprocessTransport handles submit/poll/cancel lifecycle, so custom adapters only need to build the CLI argv array
EXECUTOR_CAPABILITIES registry (src/devforge/executors/capabilities.py) separates capability metadata from adapter logic
PullPolicyRule system means context budget tuning requires no code changes, only config

roles.name: Contributor (Graph Modifier)
jobs: Add new meta-graph nodes or modify existing node functions in src/devforge/graph/nodes.py
Extend RuntimeState with new fields to carry state between nodes
Add new planning decision chains (LLM-backed deciders in src/devforge/planning/)
Implement new role specs in ROLE_REGISTRY
Write integration tests using fixture snapshots and MockLLMClient
pains: LangGraph's raw API exposes too much graph wiring detail for each new node
Testing graph-level behavior requires complex setup with real LLM calls
State management across heterogeneous graph nodes tends to become implicit dict mutation
gains: RuntimeState is a typed dataclass (src/devforge/graph/runtime_state.py:9-53) — new fields are explicit and IDE-visible
MockLLMClient (src/devforge/llm/mock.py) + fixture JSONs enable full cycle tests without network access
Planning module uses consistent decider pattern: llm_*_decider() functions with StructuredGenerationRequest/Response
256 tests serve as regression baseline — contributor knows immediately if a change breaks existing behavior
